dominant_error_pattern: Test-takers focus on the obtained score of 90 in isolation without incorporating the standard error of measurement (±6), which expands the interpretable range into below-average territory only at the lower confidence bound.
instructional_red_flags: Distractor B (average score) treats 90 as unambiguous average status without considering SEM widening. Distractor C (below-average range) ignores the upper boundary; 90+6 = 96, which is still average. Distractor D wrongly invokes percentile ranges (84th–96th) when the question requires SEM-band reasoning. Teach: SEM creates a *confidence band*, not a point. Score 90 ± 6 = 84–96, spanning below-average (84–89) to average (90–96). Correct interpretation: 'In the average to below-average area' reflects that the band touches both regions. Drill: for each score and SEM, shade the range on a normal curve and name all regions it includes.

dominant_error_pattern: Examinees conflate T-score units with percentile ranks or standard deviations, miscalculating the distance of T=64 from the mean of 50 and misidentifying the resulting normative classification.
instructional_red_flags: Distractor B (average) treats T=64 as average despite SD=10 making 64 clearly 1.4 SD above the mean. Distractor C (below-average) reverses the direction, assuming 64 is below 50. Distractor D over-pathologizes above-average performance as significantly below average. Teach: T-score = 50 + 10z, so z = (64−50)/10 = 1.4 SD above mean. Show: 1.4 SD is firmly above average (average = ±1 SD). Use a labeled T-score ruler overlaid on a normal curve to visually anchor: T=50 at center, T=60 at +1 SD, T=70 at +2 SD; T=64 falls between center and +1 SD in the above-average region.
